summary: Mobilises King’s historic commitment to knowledge with purpose through a continuous conversation with every student about their ambitions, needs and direction, helping them take ownership of their journey, connect their course with wider opportunities to learn, belong and contribute, and enabling King’s to respond to what matters to them.
detailSummary:
From accepting an offer through to graduation, every student is invited to develop and revisit a sense of what matters to them, what they hope to achieve and contribute, where they need connection or support, and how their direction is changing. Purpose spans learning, personal development, belonging, service, experience and future life, with uncertainty recognised as a legitimate starting point.

The King’s Canvas gives this individual trajectory a living form. It is a self-curated account of the experience each student wants to build, connecting their course with wider opportunities, relationships, communities and ambitions. Digital tools and carefully bounded uses of AI sustain the conversation at scale, while existing human relationships provide judgement, challenge and care at meaningful moments.

Insight into students’ ambitions and needs enables King’s to connect them with relevant opportunities and shape enrichment provision more responsively, increasing confidence in its reach, relevance and value.
problemSolved: Universities promise wider personal development but tend to understand student journeys through courses and institutional processes, leaving individual purpose implicit and underassured. At King’s, a rich but fragmented opportunity ecology is difficult to navigate and rewards students who already possess confidence, networks and institutional knowledge.
whatChanges: Every student develops a living sense of purpose and direction that connects their course with the wider King’s experience. King’s connects students with relevant opportunities and uses insight into their ambitions and needs to shape provision more responsively.

caseForChange.problem:
Universities promise more than disciplinary knowledge. They are places where students develop direction, relationships and broad capabilities as thinkers, collaborators, creators and citizens. Yet this wider developmental value remains largely implicit and underassured, treated as something students acquire through exposure rather than something every student is intentionally supported to shape.

Student journeys are also understood primarily through courses and institutional processes. The course provides an essential academic spine, but it cannot fully represent an individual trajectory that may cross disciplines or lead towards a future only loosely connected to the subject studied.

King’s has a rich ecology of academic, co-curricular and extra-curricular opportunities, but students can find it fragmented and difficult to navigate. Those with greater confidence, networks or institutional knowledge are better placed to identify what matters and how experiences connect, reinforcing inequality and fear of missing out. King’s also lacks a systematic way to understand and respond to what students want from their wider journey.
caseForChange.opportunity:
King’s can make individual purpose a visible and supported part of university life. Students can connect their course with wider ambitions for learning, belonging, development, service and future direction, while retaining the freedom to explore and change course.

Purpose includes how students relate to the world beyond themselves, the communities to which they belong and the contribution their knowledge and capabilities might enable them to make.

The King’s Canvas allows students to hold and reshape this trajectory over time. Technology supports regular reflection and relevant opportunity discovery at scale, existing relationships provide human support, and patterns in student ambitions help King’s shape provision more responsively.
caseForChange.whyNow:
Students increasingly bear the cost of higher education, with international students making a particularly significant investment. Economic, technological and cultural change is disrupting established graduate pathways and intensifying scrutiny of the graduate premium.

King’s needs to make the wider value of university intentional, visible and equitable. Students need to understand how their education, experiences and relationships combine into something personally meaningful and how they might use them to contribute.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
Every student develops a living sense of purpose and direction that connects their course with the wider King’s experience. They can make more intentional choices, revisit their trajectory as they develop and receive relevant support and opportunities.

King’s uses insight into students’ ambitions and needs to shape provision more responsively. The wider developmental value of university becomes something students can actively recognise, experience and own.
